content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Supply-side contraction of the selective track (shrink the gate, not the rules)'. Hungary cannot feasibly implement a structural reform eliminating or significantly delaying early selection before 2030 without (a) substantial prior teacher-workforce stabilization in rural and STEM areas, (b) explicit sequencing away from concurrent National Core Curriculum and AI-integration reforms, and (c) multi-party legislative consensus to protect against political reversal—only the first precondition is currently in progress.
opportunities: Shrinks the selective track's footprint without requiring statutory redefinition of the secondary-education age boundary, potentially avoiding the felmenő-rendszer and ~1-year lead-time obligations that a structural reform would trigger.

Aligns policy with the demographic reality that the exam's original rationing function is eroding, as evidenced by falling 8-year-track applications (-14% year-on-year) alongside flat overall enrolment.
strengths: Hungary's teaching workforce is structurally aging and contracting: approximately 50% of full-time primary teachers are age 50 or older, only 7.2% are under 30, and total teacher numbers have declined by over 21,000 since 2003/2004 (164,000 in 2023/2024 vs. 185,000+ in 2003/2004).

Teacher shortages in mathematics and natural sciences are acute; fewer than 3,400 people applied for university pedagogical degrees in 2024, with history–English combinations dominating demand for other subjects.
threats: Reversal requires legislative repeal since score thresholds are maintainer-level administrative practice rather than statute, offering more procedural friction than S1's decree-based mechanism, but a parliamentary majority change during the multi-year rollout still poses a significant reversal risk given the Fidesz framing of any selection-related change and the government's own pattern of rapidly reversing predecessor reforms.

Church and foundation maintainers, protected by Tisza's coalition commitment to preserve prior operating conditions, may simply expand to absorb displaced demand, neutralizing the intended contraction and creating a policy-credibility problem.
weaknesses: The actual magnitude of net positive equity gain in Hungary from delayed selection (holding school-choice and catchment rules constant) remains unknown; Polish gains may not generalize given Hungary's significantly higher baseline segregation and denser network of small rural schools.

The cost (in transition staffing, retraining, and administrative disruption) of a full or partial phase-out of early selection in Hungary is unquantified; Polish and other OECD countries' transition budgets are not published in comparable form.
